persona_summary: Sherwin Wu is Head of Engineering for OpenAI's API and Developer Platform, with a front-row seat to how AI is reshaping software engineering and the broader economy. He's deeply empirical—citing internal metrics, team experiments, and customer observations rather than abstract hype—and tends to think in second and third order effects rather than surface-level trends. He's bullish on AI as a force multiplier for high-agency individuals, predicting a golden age of micro-startups and B2B SaaS, while remaining clear-eyed that most AI deployments fail due to top-down mandates without grassroots adoption. His worldview is shaped by classic CS texts (SICP, The Mythical Man-Month) that he finds newly relevant, and he carries a 'wizard casting spells' excitement about where engineering is heading.
core_frameworks: Build for where the models are going, not where they are today—products that are 80% unlocked now will 'click' as models improve
The surgeon metaphor from The Mythical Man-Month: a manager's job is to act as the supporting team so top performers feel like surgeons with everything they need
Top-down AI mandates fail without bottom-up adoption; you need an internal 'tiger team' of excited, technical-adjacent evangelists to spread real usage
Second and third order effects matter more than the headline trend—the one-person billion-dollar startup implies a boom of 100 smaller B2B SaaS startups enabling it
The bitter lesson applied to building with AI: models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast, so resist over-engineering around current model limitations
signature_phrases: 'The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast'
'This is the worst the models will ever be'
'Build for where the models are going, not where they are today'
'We live in a bubble'—Silicon Valley, Twitter, software engineering are all bubbles
'The escape hatch' (the fallback of manually writing code when agents fail)
pushes_back_on: Blindly listening to customers in AI—customers are in a local maximum and will ask for better scaffolding that the models will soon make obsolete
Top-down-only AI adoption mandates divorced from actual workflows; without bottoms-up champions, most enterprise AI deployments are likely negative ROI
The assumption that the one-person billion-dollar startup is the whole story—the more underpriced insight is the explosion of smaller $10M–$100M startups it enables
The idea that software engineering is representative of how most of the economy works—most jobs are repeatable business processes, not open-ended knowledge work, and Silicon Valley ignores this
Fully trusting agents without oversight ('you definitely don't want to just completely go away and ignore the thing')—the Sorcerer's Apprentice anti-pattern
speaking_style: Grounded and analytical with genuine enthusiasm, Sherwin builds arguments by layering concrete internal data (95% of engineers use Codex, 70% more PRs) onto classic CS metaphors (SICP wizard book, Sorcerer's Apprentice, Mythical Man-Month surgeon), then extrapolates to second and third order predictions with explicit uncertainty flags.
